medications.indication_rationale: The friend's potent topical corticosteroid is the driver of the tinea incognito flare and MUST be ceased immediately. It suppresses local immunity, spreads the dermatophyte and thins the skin; continuing it, or adding any new topical corticosteroid, is contraindicated. Instructing its cessation is the pivotal safe action.
medications.deprescribing_note: Instruct immediate and complete cessation of the potent topical corticosteroid; warn the patient the rash may transiently look angrier/redder for 3-4 days after stopping (steroid-withdrawal rebound) before the antifungal takes effect.

medications.indication_rationale: Topical terbinafine is a first-line dermatophyte-directed antifungal for tinea cruris. Apply a thin layer to the affected area and about 2 cm of surrounding normal skin twice daily, and CONTINUE for at least 7 days after the skin looks completely clear to prevent early relapse. A topical azole (e.g. clotrimazole/miconazole) is an acceptable alternative.
medications.dose_route_frequency: terbinafine 1% cream, applied topically to the affected groin and ~2 cm beyond, twice daily, continued for 7 days after apparent clearance (dose per source note)

medications.drug_name: terbinafine 250 mg oral tablet (systemic allylamine antifungal)
medications.indication_rationale: Given the severity, the extent (spreading down the thighs), and the steroid-modification (tinea incognito), a short course of oral terbinafine is appropriate to ensure eradication, in addition to the topical agent. In the telehealth model Breath-Ezy does not itself prescribe — it directs the patient to the prescribing GP to obtain the oral course (with baseline LFTs as standard). Do NOT calculate or invent a paediatric dose; this is an adult.
medications.dose_route_frequency: terbinafine 250 mg orally once daily for 2 weeks, initiated by the prescribing GP (dose/duration per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A combined corticosteroid–antifungal cream must NOT be used here: the corticosteroid component perpetuates the tinea incognito by suppressing local immunity, which is exactly the mechanism that caused this flare. Use a corticosteroid-free antifungal.

There is no fever, no rapidly spreading hot induration beyond the rash, no fluctuance and no purulent discharge — no evidence of secondary bacterial cellulitis. Prescribing antibiotics for a raw-looking but non-infected fungal flare is an error of commission. Reserve antibiotics for if the secondary-infection safety-net triggers.

A zinc oxide barrier cream over the treated area is a reasonable adjunct to reduce friction and protect the macerated skin in the creases while it heals; supportive only, not a substitute for the antifungal.
medications.dose_route_frequency: zinc oxide barrier cream applied topically over the treated area to reduce friction (adjunct, per source note)

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the groin clean and thoroughly DRY — after showering, dry the groin last and with a SEPARATE towel from the feet, or pat dry/use a hair dryer on cool; avoid trapping moisture.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dermatophytes thrive in warm, moist, occluded skin; keeping the area dry and not sharing a towel between feet and groin removes the environment the fungus needs and prevents foot-to-groin spread.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear loose, breathable cotton boxer shorts instead of tight synthetic athletic underwear, and change out of sweaty work clothing promptly during and after heavy labouring.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing heat, sweat, friction and occlusion relieves the mechanical rubbing pain, and creates conditions in which the dermatophyte cannot flourish.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Treat the concurrent athlete's foot (tinea pedis) at the same time with an antifungal, and put socks on before underwear when dressing.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Untreated tinea pedis is a reservoir that re-infects the groin (foot-to-groin autoinoculation); treating it and dressing feet-first prevents relapse.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common fungal groin infection (tinea) that flared up badly because the strong steroid cream suppressed your skin's local defences and let the fungus spread — so the single most important step is to STOP that steroid cream completely.
For the first 3-4 days after stopping the steroid the rash may look angrier and redder before it settles — that is expected steroid rebound, not the antifungal failing.
Keep using the antifungal cream twice a day and continue it for a full 7 days AFTER the skin looks completely clear, to stop it coming straight back.
The brown discolouration left behind will take months to fade and is not the infection persisting.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP appointment within a few days to obtain the oral antifungal course (with baseline LFTs) and confirm the diagnosis/steroid cessation; routine GP review in about 2 weeks to confirm resolution
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response to antifungal therapy and cessation of the steroid; any steroid-induced skin thinning; exclusion of secondary bacterial infection; whether mycology (KOH/culture) is needed if the picture is atypical or not settling; adherence to hygiene/clothing measures and treatment of the tinea pedis
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading hot tender swelling beyond the rash, fever or chills, thick foul-smelling yellow/green pus, or the rash worsening rather than improving after several days of correct treatment

must_include_items: Recognise a common groin dermatophyte infection (tinea) whose acute flare is driven by inappropriate potent-topical-corticosteroid use (tinea incognito) — NOT an emergency and NOT candidiasis
Instruct IMMEDIATE and complete cessation of the potent topical corticosteroid (and do not add any new corticosteroid or a steroid–antifungal combination)

Recommend a topical dermatophyte-directed antifungal (e.g. terbinafine 1% cream twice daily, continued 7 days after clearance), and arrange oral antifungal therapy (oral terbinafine) via the prescribing GP given the severity/extent
Give drying/hygiene and loose-cotton-clothing advice and advise treating the concurrent athlete's foot to prevent re-infection
Safety-net explicitly for secondary bacterial infection (spreading hot swelling, fever/chills, foul pus) and for failure to improve — escalate to in-person GP / urgent care if these appear
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a topical azole antifungal (clotrimazole or miconazole) instead of terbinafine is an acceptable first-line choice.

Starting with topical antifungal + steroid cessation + hygiene advice and arranging routine GP follow-up (deferring the decision on oral terbinafine to the GP) is an acceptable, non-defect management pathway.
Arranging a routine (rather than urgent) telehealth or in-person GP review to obtain the oral script and baseline LFTs is acceptable.
errors_of_omission: Failing to identify and instruct cessation of the potent topical corticosteroid
Failing to recommend any dermatophyte-directed antifungal
Failing to safety-net for secondary bacterial infection / treatment failure
Ignoring the concurrent tinea pedis reservoir and the sweat/friction/occlusion factors
errors_of_commission: Continuing or newly recommending a potent topical corticosteroid, or prescribing a combined corticosteroid–antifungal cream (perpetuating tinea incognito)
Prescribing systemic antibiotics for a non-infected fungal flare
Anchoring on candidiasis and giving anticandidal-only therapy (e.g. nystatin)
Over-triaging a stable, afebrile, cellulitis-negative tinea to the emergency department or a 000 ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) instructing cessation of the potent topical corticosteroid AND (b) recommending an appropriate dermatophyte-directed antifungal, together with secondary-infection safety-netting, WITHOUT over-triaging a stable tinea to emergency care and WITHOUT continuing a topical steroid / steroid–antifungal combination or giving antibiotics. Over-triage to ED/000 and perpetuating the steroid are the two scored failure modes.
